 {"id":"caam-8y9","title":"Implement CLI sync commands","description":"# Task: Implement CLI sync commands

## Context
Implement the CLI commands for managing the sync pool and triggering synchronization.

## Commands

### `caam sync`
Sync now with all machines in pool.

```bash
caam sync [flags]

Flags:
  --machine NAME    Sync only with specific machine
  --provider PROV   Sync only specific provider
  --profile PROF    Sync only specific profile
  --dry-run         Show what would sync without doing it
  --force           Force sync even if recently synced
  --json            Output results as JSON
```

Output:
```
Syncing with 3 machines...

  work-laptop (192.168.1.100):
    ✓ claude/alice@gmail.com: pushed (local fresher)
    ✓ claude/bob@gmail.com: pulled (remote fresher)
    ✓ codex/work@company.com: up to date
    
  home-desktop (10.0.0.50):
    ✓ claude/alice@gmail.com: pushed
    ✓ gemini/personal@gmail.com: pulled
    
  cloud-vm (34.123.45.67):
    ✗ Connection failed: timeout

Sync complete: 4 pushed, 2 pulled, 1 up to date, 1 error
```

### `caam sync init` ⭐ NEW
Interactive first-time setup wizard:

```bash
caam sync init [flags]

Flags:
  --discover    Auto-discover from SSH config
  --csv         Create CSV template only
```

Flow:
```
$ caam sync init

Welcome to CAAM Sync Setup!

This will help you set up syncing between your machines.

Step 1: Create your sync pool

  Discovered hosts from ~/.ssh/config:
    [1] work-laptop (192.168.1.100)
    [2] home-desktop (10.0.0.50)
    [3] cloud-vm (34.123.45.67)
    [a] Add all discovered hosts
    [m] Manually add a machine
    [s] Skip for now

  Choice: a

Step 2: Test connectivity

  Testing work-laptop... ✓ OK (23ms)
  Testing home-desktop... ✓ OK (45ms)  
  Testing cloud-vm... ✗ Failed: connection refused

  1 machine failed. Continue anyway? (Y/n): y

Step 3: Enable auto-sync?

  Auto-sync automatically pushes fresh tokens when you backup or refresh.
  
  Enable auto-sync? (y/N): y

Setup complete!
  Machines in pool: 3 (2 online, 1 offline)
  Auto-sync: enabled

Run 'caam sync' to sync now, or it will happen automatically.
```

### `caam sync status`
Show sync pool status and last sync times.

```bash
caam sync status [flags]

Flags:
  --json    Output as JSON
```

Output:
```
Sync Status

Local Machine: jeffs-macbook
Auto-sync: enabled
Last full sync: 2025-12-19 14:29:00

Machines in pool:
  NAME           ADDRESS          STATUS    LAST SYNC
  work-laptop    192.168.1.100    🟢 online  5 min ago
  home-desktop   10.0.0.50        🟢 online  5 min ago
  cloud-vm       34.123.45.67     🔴 offline 2 hours ago (timeout)

Profiles tracked: 8
  claude: 5 profiles
  codex: 2 profiles
  gemini: 1 profile

Queue: 0 pending | History: 127 entries
```

### `caam sync add`
Add a machine to the sync pool.

```bash
caam sync add <name> <address> [flags]

Flags:
  --key PATH        Path to SSH private key (default: auto-detect)
  --user NAME       SSH username (default: current user)
  --remote-path P   Path to caam data on remote (default: same as local)
  --test            Test connectivity after adding
  --no-test         Skip connectivity test
```

Examples:
```bash
# Basic add (auto-detects SSH key)
caam sync add work-laptop 192.168.1.100

# With specific key and test
caam sync add cloud-vm 34.123.45.67 --key ~/.ssh/cloud_key --test

# With port and user in address
caam sync add dev-server jeff@dev.example.com:2222
```

### `caam sync remove`
Remove a machine from the sync pool.

```bash
caam sync remove <name> [flags]

Flags:
  --force    Skip confirmation
```

### `caam sync test`
Test connectivity to one or all machines.

```bash
caam sync test [name] [flags]

Flags:
  --all     Test all machines in pool
```

Output for single machine:
```
Testing connection to work-laptop (192.168.1.100)...
  SSH connection: ✓ OK (latency: 23ms)
  SFTP subsystem: ✓ OK
  CAAM data path: ✓ Found (~/.local/share/caam)
  CAAM version: v1.2.3 (compatible)
  Profiles found: 5
Connection successful!
```

Output for --all:
```
Testing all machines...
  work-laptop:   ✓ OK (23ms, 5 profiles)
  home-desktop:  ✓ OK (45ms, 3 profiles)
  cloud-vm:      ✗ Failed: connection refused

2/3 machines online
```

### `caam sync enable` / `caam sync disable`
Enable or disable auto-sync.

```bash
caam sync enable     # Enable auto-sync after backup/refresh
caam sync disable    # Disable auto-sync
```

### `caam sync log`
Show sync history.

```bash
caam sync log [flags]

Flags:
  --limit N     Show last N entries (default: 20)
  --machine M   Filter by machine
  --provider P  Filter by provider
  --errors      Show only errors
  --json        Output as JSON
```

Output:
```
Sync History (last 20 entries)

TIME                 MACHINE        PROFILE                  ACTION   STATUS
2025-12-19 14:29:00  work-laptop    claude/alice@gmail.com   push     ✓
2025-12-19 14:28:55  work-laptop    codex/work@company.com   pull     ✓
2025-12-19 14:28:50  home-desktop   claude/bob@gmail.com     push     ✓
2025-12-19 12:15:30  cloud-vm       *                        sync     ✗ timeout
...
```

### `caam sync discover`
Discover machines from SSH config.

```bash
caam sync discover [flags]

Flags:
  --add       Add discovered machines to pool
  --test      Test connectivity to discovered machines
```

Output:
```
Discovered machines from ~/.ssh/config:

  NAME           ADDRESS          KEY                   STATUS
  work-laptop    192.168.1.100    ~/.ssh/work_key      not in pool
  home-desktop   10.0.0.50        ~/.ssh/id_ed25519    not in pool
  github         github.com       (skipped: git host)

Add discovered machines to sync pool? (y/N): y

Added 2 machines. Run 'caam sync test --all' to verify connectivity.
```

### `caam sync edit`
Open sync config (CSV file) in editor.

```bash
caam sync edit
```

Opens `~/.caam/sync_machines.csv` in $EDITOR (or sensible default).

### `caam sync queue`
Manage the retry queue.

```bash
caam sync queue [flags]

Flags:
  --clear     Clear all pending retries
  --process   Process pending retries now
  --json      Output as JSON
```

Output:
```
Sync Queue: 2 pending

  PROFILE                  MACHINE       ATTEMPTS   LAST ERROR
  claude/alice@gmail.com   cloud-vm      2          connection refused
  codex/work@company.com   cloud-vm      1          timeout

Use 'caam sync queue --process' to retry now.
Use 'caam sync queue --clear' to clear the queue.
```

```go
// internal/authpool/pool.go

type PooledProfile struct {
    Provider     string
    ProfileName  string
    TokenExpiry  time.Time
    LastRefresh  time.Time
    LastCheck    time.Time
    Status       PoolStatus
    ErrorCount   int
    ErrorMessage string
}

type PoolStatus int
const (
    PoolStatusUnknown PoolStatus = iota
    PoolStatusReady      // Token valid, ready to use
    PoolStatusRefreshing // Currently refreshing
    PoolStatusExpired    // Token expired, needs refresh
    PoolStatusError      // Refresh failed
)

type AuthPool struct {
    profiles map[string]*PooledProfile
    mu       sync.RWMutex
    
    refreshThreshold time.Duration
    checkInterval    time.Duration
    maxRetries       int
    
    vault     *authfile.Vault
    refresher map[string]refresh.Refresher
}
```

## Overview
Integrate the AuthPool with the existing daemon infrastructure so it runs in the background.

## Technical Details

### Daemon Enhancement
```go
// internal/daemon/daemon.go

type Daemon struct {
    watcher  *watcher.Watcher
    authPool *authpool.AuthPool  // NEW
}

func (d *Daemon) Start(ctx context.Context) error {
    g, ctx := errgroup.WithContext(ctx)
    
    g.Go(func() error {
        return d.watcher.Watch(ctx)
    })
    
    // NEW: Start auth pool monitor
    if d.authPool != nil {
        g.Go(func() error {
            d.authPool.MonitorLoop(ctx)
            return nil
        })
    }
    
    return g.Wait()
}
```

- caam pool status: Show pool state
- caam pool refresh [provider/profile]: Force refresh
- caam pool enable/disable: Toggle pool monitoring

## Purpose
Implement OAuth token refresh for Google Gemini using standard Google OAuth2 flow.

## Reference (from codex-pool)
```go
func (h *proxyHandler) refreshGeminiAccount(ctx context.Context, a *Account, 
    refreshTok string) error {
    form := url.Values{}
    form.Set("client_id", geminiOAuthClientID())
    form.Set("grant_type", "refresh_token")
    form.Set("refresh_token", refreshTok)
    // POST to https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token
}
```

**KEY DIFFERENCE**: Gemini uses form-encoded body, NOT JSON!

## Technical Requirements

### Constants
```go
const (
    GeminiTokenURL = "https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token"
)

// Client ID from gcloud ADC or gemini CLI
func geminiClientID() string {
    // This may need to be read from the ADC file

}
```

### Auth File Location
```
~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json
```

### ADC File Format
```json
{
  "client_id": "764086051850-6qr4p6gpi6hn506pt8ejuq83di341hur.apps.googleusercontent.com",
  "client_secret": "d-FL95Q19q7MQmFpd7hHD0Ty",
  "refresh_token": "1//...",
  "type": "authorized_user"
}
```

**Note**: ADC file contains client_id and client_secret - use these for refresh!

### Refresh Function
```go
func RefreshGeminiToken(ctx context.Context, clientID, clientSecret, refreshToken string) (*TokenResponse, error) {
    form := url.Values{}
    form.Set("client_id", clientID)
    form.Set("client_secret", clientSecret)
    form.Set("grant_type", "refresh_token")
    form.Set("refresh_token", refreshToken)
    
    req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, "POST", GeminiTokenURL, 
        strings.NewReader(form.Encode()))
    req.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/x-www-form-urlencoded")
    
    resp, err := http.DefaultClient.Do(req)
    if err != nil {
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("gemini refresh failed: %w", err)
    }
    defer resp.Body.Close()
    
    if resp.StatusCode != 200 {
        body, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
        return nil, fmt.Errorf("gemini refresh error %d: %s", 
            resp.StatusCode, string(body))
    }
    
    var tokenResp TokenResponse
    json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&tokenResp)
    
    return &tokenResp, nil
}
```

### Google TokenResponse
```go
// Google returns slightly different format
type GoogleTokenResponse struct {
    AccessToken  string `json:"access_token"`
    ExpiresIn    int    `json:"expires_in"`    // Seconds
    Scope        string `json:"scope"`
    TokenType    string `json:"token_type"`
    // Note: refresh_token is NOT returned on refresh
}
```

### File Update Approach
For Gemini/gcloud ADC, we typically do NOT update the ADC file. Instead:
1. Store the access token separately for caam use
2. Or update health metadata with expiry only
3. The ADC file is managed by gcloud CLI

```go
func UpdateGeminiHealth(provider, profile string, resp *TokenResponse) error {
    // Update health metadata only
    health := healthStore.GetProfile(provider, profile)
    health.TokenExpiresAt = time.Now().Add(
        time.Duration(resp.ExpiresIn) * time.Second)
    return healthStore.UpdateProfile(provider, profile, health)
}
```

## Alternative: Read-Only Approach
Since gcloud manages ADC, we might just:
1. Read expiry from access token (if JWT)
2. Parse token to check expiry
3. Recommend user run `gcloud auth login` if expired

 {"id":"caam-9y2","title":"Export/Import Vault Bundle","description":"# Epic: Export/Import Vault Bundle

## Overview
Create a single-command export that bundles the entire vault into a portable zip file, and a corresponding import command to restore it on another machine.

## Background
Users with multiple machines need to transfer their auth profiles. Currently this requires manual copying of directories. A single zip bundle makes this trivial and ensures nothing is missed.

## User Story
As a user with multiple machines, I want to export all my saved auth profiles to a single file, so that I can easily set up caam on another machine with all my accounts.

## Filename Format
`Exported_Coding_Agent_Account_Auth_Info__As_of__MM_DD_YYYY__HH_MM_AM.zip`

Example: `Exported_Coding_Agent_Account_Auth_Info__As_of__12_19_2025__02_29_PM.zip`

## Bundle Contents
```
export_bundle/
├── manifest.json          # Export metadata, checksums, version
├── vault/                 # All provider profiles
│   ├── claude/
│   │   ├── alice@gmail.com/
│   │   └── bob@gmail.com/
│   ├── codex/
│   └── gemini/
├── config.yaml            # User configuration (optional)
├── projects.json          # Project associations (optional)
├── health.json            # Health metadata (optional)
└── caam.db                # Activity database (optional)
```

## Manifest Schema
```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "caam_version": "1.2.3",
  "export_timestamp": "2025-12-19T14:29:00Z",
  "source_machine": "hostname",
  "source_platform": "darwin_arm64",
  "profiles": {
    "claude": ["alice@gmail.com", "bob@gmail.com"],
    "codex": ["work@company.com"],
    "gemini": []
  },
  "includes": {
    "config": true,
    "projects": true,
    "health": true,
    "database": false
  },
  "checksums": {
    "vault/claude/alice@gmail.com/.claude.json": "sha256:abc123..."
  }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
### Export
- [ ] `caam export [--output PATH]` creates zip bundle
- [ ] TUI export action (key 'E')
- [ ] Automatic filename with timestamp
- [ ] Includes vault, optionally config/projects/health/db
- [ ] Manifest with checksums for integrity verification
- [ ] Progress indicator for large exports
- [ ] Success message shows path to created file

### Import
- [ ] `caam import <bundle.zip>` restores from bundle
- [ ] TUI import action (key 'I')
- [ ] Validates manifest and checksums
- [ ] Handles version compatibility
- [ ] Merge mode: add profiles that don't exist, skip existing
- [ ] Replace mode: overwrite everything
- [ ] Preview mode: show what will be imported without doing it
- [ ] Handles cross-platform path differences

## Technical Notes
- Use Go's archive/zip for bundle creation
- Paths in zip must be relative, not absolute
- Cross-platform: handle ~/.local/share/caam vs %APPDATA%\caam
- Consider optional encryption (AES-256) for sensitive tokens?

## Security Considerations
- Bundle contains bearer tokens - warn user about securing the file
- Delete bundle after import? Or leave for user to manage
- Don't include SSH keys or other unrelated secrets

## Dependencies
None - standalone feature

## Effort Estimate
Medium - new subsystem but straightforward file I/O","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-19T14:38:09.146772172-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-19T16:58:32.06150765-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-19T16:58:32.06150765-05:00","close_reason":"Export/Import Vault Bundle epic complete: CLI export (caam-x4j), CLI import (caam-103), TUI export/import (caam-gju), and bundle format (caam-4b5) all implemented and tested."}

 {"id":"caam-a207","title":"Implement unified configuration for thresholds and patterns","description":"# Task: Implement unified configuration for thresholds and patterns

## Overview
Create a configuration system for user-customizable settings like alert thresholds, rate limit patterns, login success/failure patterns, and notification preferences.

## Why This Matters
Currently scattered across code:
- Rate limit detection patterns in ratelimit/detector.go
- Login patterns will be in handoff handlers
- Alert thresholds as constants

Problems:
- Users cant customize without code changes
- Provider CLI updates break detection
- No way to tune for specific workflows

## Technical Details

### Config File Location
`~/.caam/config.yaml` (already used for some settings)

### Config Structure
```yaml
# ~/.caam/config.yaml

# Alert and rotation settings
alerts:
  enabled: true
  warning_threshold: 70    # Percent
  critical_threshold: 85
  notifications:
    terminal: true
    desktop: true
    webhook: ""

# Smart handoff settings
handoff:
  auto_trigger: true       # false = prompt user first
  debounce_delay: 2s       # Wait before triggering
  max_retries: 1           # Max handoff attempts per session
  fallback_to_manual: true # Show manual instructions on failure

# Rate limit detection patterns (regex)
rate_limit_patterns:
  claude:
    - "rate limit"
    - "usage limit reached"
    - "too many requests"
    - "429"
  codex:
    - "rate limit exceeded"
    - "quota exceeded"
  gemini:
    - "RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED"
    - "quota exceeded"

# Login success/failure patterns (regex)
login_patterns:
  claude:
    success:
      - "successfully logged in"
      - "authenticated"
      - "login complete"
    failure:
      - "authentication failed"
      - "invalid token"
      - "expired"
  codex:
    success: [...]
    failure: [...]

# Subscription costs (for cost analysis)
subscriptions:
  claude:
    plan: "max"
    monthly_cost: 200
  codex:
    plan: "pro"
    monthly_cost: 20

# Daemon settings
daemon:
  auth_pool:
    enabled: true
    check_interval: 5m
    refresh_threshold: 15m
    max_retries: 3
```

 {"id":"caam-i5j","title":"Signal handler for graceful operations","description":"## Purpose
Implement signal handling for SIGHUP-triggered reload and graceful shutdown.

## Background
Unix signals provide a standard way to communicate with running processes.
SIGHUP traditionally means "reload configuration".
SIGINT/SIGTERM mean "shutdown gracefully".

## Signals to Handle

| Signal | Action | Use Case |
|--------|--------|----------|
| SIGHUP | Reload profiles | External script triggered refresh |
| SIGINT | Graceful shutdown | Ctrl+C in terminal |
| SIGTERM | Graceful shutdown | Process manager (systemd, docker) |
| SIGUSR1 | Dump stats to log | Debugging/monitoring |

## Technical Implementation

### Signal Handler Package
Create internal/signals/signals.go with Handler struct that listens for
signals and exposes channels for reload, shutdown, and debug events.

### TUI Integration
Add watchSignals() tea.Cmd that listens to the signal handler channels
and emits appropriate messages (reloadRequestedMsg, tea.Quit, dumpStatsMsg).

### CLI for Sending Signals
Add `caam reload` command that finds TUI PID and sends SIGHUP.

### PID File Management
Write PID file on TUI start, remove on clean shutdown.

## Files to Create
- internal/signals/signals.go
- internal/signals/signals_test.go
- cmd/caam/reload.go

## Platform Considerations
- SIGHUP/SIGUSR1 not available on Windows
- Use build tags for platform-specific implementations
- Windows alternative: named pipe or file-based signaling

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SIGHUP triggers profile reload in TUI
- [ ] SIGINT/SIGTERM cause graceful shutdown

-{"id":"caam-ihiw","title":"Unit tests for internal/handoff login handlers","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for internal/handoff Login Handlers

## Overview
Comprehensive unit tests for provider-specific login handlers that detect login commands, success patterns, and failure patterns.

## Test Categories

### 1. Claude Login Handler Tests
```go
func TestClaudeLoginHandler(t *testing.T) {
    handler := handoff.NewClaudeLoginHandler()
    
    t.Run("trigger_login", func(t *testing.T) {
        t.Log("[TEST] Testing login command trigger")
        mockPTY := NewMockPTY()
        
        err := handler.TriggerLogin(mockPTY)
        
        t.Logf("[TEST] Injected commands: %v", mockPTY.InjectedCommands())
        require.NoError(t, err)
        require.Contains(t, mockPTY.InjectedCommands(), "/login")
    })
    
    t.Run("detect_success", func(t *testing.T) {
        tests := []struct {
            output    string
            isSuccess bool
        }{
            {"Successfully logged in as alice@gmail.com", true},
            {"Authenticated successfully", true},
            {"Login complete", true},
            {"Error: authentication failed", false},
            {"Still processing...", false},
        }
        
        for _, tc := range tests {
            t.Logf("[TEST] Output: %q", tc.output)
            result := handler.IsLoginComplete(tc.output)
            t.Logf("[TEST] IsComplete: %v (want %v)", result, tc.isSuccess)
            require.Equal(t, tc.isSuccess, result)
        }
    })
    
    t.Run("detect_failure", func(t *testing.T) {
        tests := []struct {
            output   string
            isFailed bool
            errMsg   string
        }{
            {"Error: token expired", true, "token expired"},
            {"Authentication failed: invalid credentials", true, "invalid credentials"},
            {"Login successful", false, ""},
        }
        
        for _, tc := range tests {
            t.Logf("[TEST] Output: %q", tc.output)
            failed, msg := handler.IsLoginFailed(tc.output)
            t.Logf("[TEST] Failed=%v, Msg=%q", failed, msg)
            require.Equal(t, tc.isFailed, failed)
            if tc.isFailed {
                require.Contains(t, msg, tc.errMsg)
            }
        }
    })
}
```

### 2. Codex Login Handler Tests
Similar structure but with Codex-specific patterns:
- Trigger: May use different command
- Success: Codex-specific success messages
- Failure: Codex-specific error messages

### 3. Gemini Login Handler Tests
Similar structure but with Gemini-specific patterns:
- Trigger: /auth or similar
- Success: Google OAuth success patterns
- Failure: Google-specific errors

### 4. Handler Registry Tests
```go
func TestHandlerRegistry(t *testing.T) {
    providers := []string{"claude", "codex", "gemini"}
    
    for _, p := range providers {
        t.Run(p, func(t *testing.T) {
            t.Logf("[TEST] Getting handler for: %s", p)
            
            handler := handoff.GetHandler(p)
            
            t.Logf("[TEST] Handler: %T", handler)
            require.NotNil(t, handler)
            require.Equal(t, p, handler.Provider())
        })
    }
    
    t.Run("unknown_provider", func(t *testing.T) {
        handler := handoff.GetHandler("unknown")
        require.Nil(t, handler)
    })
}
```

### 5. Pattern Edge Cases
- Partial match (output split across reads)
- Case sensitivity
- Unicode in output
- ANSI escape codes in output

 {"id":"caam-ir7","title":"Project-profile association storage","description":"## Purpose
Create storage system for project-profile associations.

## Background
codex-pool pins conversations to accounts for context continuity.
For caam, we adapt this as "project-profile associations" - remembering which
profile a user prefers for each project directory.

This enables workflows like:
- Freelancers with multiple client accounts
- Work/personal separation by directory
- Team projects using team accounts

## Storage Format

### File Location
~/.caam/projects.json

### Schema
```json
{
  "version": 1,
  "associations": {
    "/home/user/work/client-a": {
      "claude": "client-a@work.com",
      "codex": "work-main"
    },
    "/home/user/personal/blog": {
      "claude": "personal@gmail.com"
    }
  },
  "defaults": {
    "claude": "personal@gmail.com"
  }
}
```

## Technical Implementation

### Storage Interface
Create internal/project/store.go with ProjectStore struct that handles
loading, saving, and querying project associations.

### Path Matching
Support exact paths and glob patterns. Check parent directories if exact
match not found (inheritance).

### Atomic Writes
Use temp file + rename pattern for thread-safe updates (same pattern as
existing profile storage).

## Files to Create
- internal/project/store.go
- internal/project/store_test.go
- internal/project/matcher.go (path matching logic)

- [ ] Load and save project associations
- [ ] Query by directory path
- [ ] Parent directory inheritance works
- [ ] Atomic file writes
- [ ] Handle missing/corrupt file gracefully
- [ ] Version field for future migrations","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-17T16:25:32.022502289-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-17T17:38:44.186281392-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-17T17:38:44.186281392-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented project-profile association storage (projects.json) with inheritance + glob matching, atomic writes, and tests.","comments":[{"id":1,"issue_id":"caam-ir7","author":"BlueBear","text":"Claimed by BlueBear.  {"id":"caam-jwgx.1","title":"Desktop Notifications for Token Events","description":"## Overview
System notifications for token expiry, rate limits, and background operations.

## ⚠️ Priority Note: P3 (Backlog)
Desktop notifications add significant complexity:
- External dependency (beeep or similar library)
- Cross-platform testing (macOS, Linux, Windows)
- Daemon integration
- Notification throttling logic
- User preference management

Consider this a polish feature after core functionality is solid.

## Problem Statement
The daemon runs in background doing token refresh and health checks, but has no way to alert users when something needs attention.

## Alternative Approaches Worth Considering

### Alternative 1: CLI Warning on Next Run
Instead of push notifications, show warnings when user runs any caam command:
```
$ caam ls
⚠️ Warning: Claude profile 'work' token expires in 2 hours
   Run: caam refresh claude work
```
This is simpler and doesn't require external dependencies.

### Alternative 2: Status File
Write a status file the user's shell prompt can check:
```
~/.local/share/caam/status.json
```
Shell integration can show alerts in prompt. Already have shell integration from caam-k9z.

### Alternative 3: True Desktop Notifications
Full beeep integration as originally planned.

## If Implementing Full Notifications

### Design Decisions
- Use `beeep` library for cross-platform support
- Config controls notification levels:
```yaml
notifications:
  enabled: true
  levels:
    critical: true    # Token expired, auth failure
    warning: true     # Token expiring soon, high penalty
    info: false       # Sync complete, backup complete
```
- Rate limit notifications (max 1 per minute per type)
- Respect system DND/Focus modes where possible

### Implementation Tasks
1. Add notifications package using beeep
2. Add NotificationConfig to config package
3. Integrate with daemon for token expiry checks
4. Integrate with wrap for rate limit events
5. Add `caam notify test` command
6. Add notification throttling

## Recommendation
Start with Alternative 1 (CLI warnings) which provides most value with minimal complexity. Only pursue full desktop notifications if users explicitly request it.

## Files to Create/Modify (if full implementation)
- internal/notifications/notify.go - New package
- internal/config/notifications.go - Config
- internal/daemon/daemon.go - Integration
- internal/wrap/wrap.go - Integration
- cmd/caam/cmd/notify.go - Test command","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"BlackMountain","created_at":"2025-12-19T23:38:35.696110786-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T00:12:44.038649218-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T00:12:44.038649218-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented Alternative 1 (CLI warnings): created internal/warnings package, integrated into root.go PersistentPreRunE, shows warnings for expiring tokens when running commands (excludes version, paths, validate, doctor, JSON mode, non-terminal)","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"caam-jwgx.1","depends_on_id":"caam-jwgx","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-19T23:38:35.697253465-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"caam-k9z","title":"Shell integration: prompt, completion, auto-activate","description":"## Overview
Deep shell integration that makes caam invisible during daily use. Users type familiar commands (claude, codex) and caam handles everything behind the scenes.

## The Problem
Users must consciously use caam commands:
```bash
caam activate claude work
claude "help me"
# ... later ...
caam next claude
claude "continue"
```

This requires:
- Remembering to use caam
- Knowing current profile state
- Manual switching

## The Solution
Shell integration with three components:

### 1. Smart Aliases (caam shell-init)
```bash
# User adds to .bashrc/.zshrc:
eval "$(caam shell-init)"

# This creates functions that wrap the AI CLIs:
# claude() { caam run claude "$@"; }
# codex() { caam run codex "$@"; }
# gemini() { caam run gemini "$@"; }
```

Now `claude "prompt"` automatically:
- Uses the best available profile
- Handles rate limits transparently
- Records usage for analytics

### 2. Tab Completion
```bash
caam activate cl<TAB>     → claude
caam activate claude w<TAB> → work-account-1
caam switch <TAB>         → [shows all profiles with health]
```

Uses Cobra's built-in completion + dynamic profile completion.

### 3. Prompt Integration (Optional)
Show current profile in shell prompt:
```bash
# In PS1/PROMPT:
alice@work ~/code (claude:work-1) $

# Or as right-prompt showing all:
                                [claude:work codex:main]
```

### 4. Auto-Activation (direnv-style)
```bash
cd ~/work/backend
# caam: Activated workspace 'work' for this directory
#       claude: work-account
#       codex: work-codex

cd ~/personal/side-project  
# caam: Activated workspace 'personal'
#       claude: personal
```

## Technical Design

### Shell Init Command
`caam shell-init [bash|zsh|fish]` outputs shell-specific code:

**Bash/Zsh:**
```bash
# Wrapper functions
claude() { caam run claude "$@"; }
codex() { caam run codex "$@"; }
gemini() { caam run gemini "$@"; }

# Completion
eval "$(caam completion bash)"

# Prompt helper (optional)
_caam_prompt() {
  caam status --format=prompt 2>/dev/null
}

# Auto-activation hook (optional)
_caam_chpwd() {
  caam project check --quiet
}
chpwd_functions+=(_caam_chpwd)  # zsh
# or PROMPT_COMMAND for bash
```

**Fish:**
```fish
function claude; caam run claude $argv; end
function codex; caam run codex $argv; end
# etc.
```

### Dynamic Completion
Profile names must be completed dynamically:
```go
// In cmd/caam/cmd/activate.go
ValidArgsFunction: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string, toComplete string) ([]string, cobra.ShellCompDirective) {
    if len(args) == 0 {
        // Complete tool names
        return []string{"claude", "codex", "gemini"}, cobra.ShellCompDirectiveNoFileComp
    }
    // Complete profile names for the given tool
    profiles := vault.List(args[0])
    return profiles, cobra.ShellCompDirectiveNoFileComp
}
```

### Prompt Status
`caam status --format=prompt` returns minimal string:
```
claude:work codex:main
# or with health indicators:
claude:work(🟢) codex:main(🟡)
```

### Auto-Activation Hook
When directory changes:
1. Check if current dir (or parents) have `.caam/` config
2. If workspace defined, activate it
3. If profile mappings defined, activate per-tool
4. Cache result to avoid repeated lookups

## Configuration
```yaml
shell:
  auto_aliases: true           # Create wrapper functions
  completion: true             # Enable tab completion
  prompt_integration: false    # Show in prompt (opt-in)
  auto_activate: true          # direnv-style activation
  
  # Prompt format
  prompt_format: "caam:%t:%p"  # %t=tool, %p=profile
  prompt_show_health: true     # Include health indicator
```

## User Experience

### Installation
```bash
$ caam shell-init --install
Adding to ~/.zshrc:
  eval "$(caam shell-init zsh)"

Restart your shell or run:
  source ~/.zshrc

Done! Now 'claude', 'codex', 'gemini' will use caam automatically.
```

### Daily Use
```bash
$ claude "fix this bug"
# Just works - caam picks best profile, handles limits

$ cd ~/work/backend
# caam: Using workspace 'work'

$ claude "continue the refactor"
# Uses work profile automatically
```

### Completion Demo
```bash
$ caam activate <TAB>
claude    codex    gemini

$ caam activate claude <TAB>
work-1         (🟢 active, expires 23h)
work-2         (🟡 expires 2h)
personal       (🔴 in cooldown)
```

## Implementation Tasks
1. **Shell init command** (cmd/caam/cmd/shell_init.go)
   - Generate shell-specific init code
   - Support bash, zsh, fish
   - --install flag for auto-add to rc file

2. **Dynamic completion** (cmd/caam/cmd/completion.go)
   - Profile name completion
   - Health indicators in completion descriptions
   - Tool name completion

3. **Prompt helper** (cmd/caam/cmd/status.go)
   - --format=prompt flag
   - Minimal output for PS1

4. **Auto-activation hook** (cmd/caam/cmd/project.go)
   - project check --quiet command
   - Workspace activation logic

## Success Criteria
- [ ] `eval "$(caam shell-init)"` works for bash/zsh/fish
- [ ] Tab completion works for all commands
- [ ] Profile names complete with health hints
- [ ] Prompt integration is optional but works
- [ ] Auto-activation triggers on cd (zsh chpwd, bash PROMPT_COMMAND)
- [ ] Performance: shell init adds <10ms to shell startup

## Dependencies
- Cobra completion (built-in)
- Wrapper mode (caam-q9f) for the aliases to work

## Priority: P1 (High Value)
This turns caam into an invisible layer, which is the goal of Zero-Friction UX.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-19T18:59:02.491764838-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-19T20:51:05.916678255-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-19T20:51:05.916678255-05:00","close_reason":"Shell integration implemented: caam shell init command generates bash/zsh/fish wrapper functions and tab completions. ## Overview
Enhance the exec.Runner to detect rate limits in real-time output and trigger a callback for handling.

## Background
The current caam run command uses internal/ratelimit/detector.go to check for rate limit patterns. We need to integrate this with the PTY controller to enable real-time detection during execution.

## Technical Details

### Enhanced Runner Options
```go
// internal/exec/runner.go

type RunOptions struct {
    // ... existing fields ...
    
    // NEW: Smart handoff options
    OnRateLimit     func(ctx context.Context) error  // Callback when rate limit detected
    RateLimitDelay  time.Duration                     // Debounce time before triggering
}
```

### Output Monitoring
```go
func (r *Runner) monitorOutput(ctx context.Context, reader io.Reader) {
    scanner := bufio.NewScanner(reader)
    for scanner.Scan() {
        line := scanner.Text()
        
        // Pass through to actual stdout
        fmt.Println(line)
        
        // Check for rate limit
        if r.detector.Check(line) {
            if r.opts.OnRateLimit \!= nil {
                go r.opts.OnRateLimit(ctx)
            }
        }
    }
}
```

## Dependencies
- Create PTY controller for command injection

 {"id":"caam-l3w7","title":"Unit tests for internal/authpool package","description":"# Task: Unit Tests for internal/authpool Package

## Overview
Comprehensive unit tests for the AuthPool manager, token monitor loop, and profile state management.

## Test Categories

### 1. Pool Initialization Tests
```go
func TestPoolCreation(t *testing.T) {
    t.Log("[TEST] Creating empty pool")
    pool := authpool.NewAuthPool(vault)
    t.Logf("[TEST] Pool created with %d profiles", pool.Count())
    
    t.Log("[TEST] Adding profile")
    err := pool.AddProfile("claude", "alice@gmail.com")
    t.Logf("[TEST] Add result: err=%v", err)
}
```

### 2. Token Expiry Detection Tests
- Token expires in future (should be Ready)
- Token expires soon (should trigger refresh)
- Token already expired (should be Expired)
- Token with no expiry info (should be Unknown)

### 3. Refresh Logic Tests
```go
func TestRefreshTrigger(t *testing.T) {
    t.Log("[TEST] Setting up profile with 10min TTL")
    profile := &PooledProfile{
        TokenExpiry: time.Now().Add(10 * time.Minute),
    }
    
    pool := NewAuthPool(WithRefreshThreshold(15 * time.Minute))
    
    t.Log("[TEST] Checking if refresh needed")
    needsRefresh := pool.NeedsRefresh(profile)
    t.Logf("[TEST] TTL=10min, Threshold=15min, NeedsRefresh=%v", needsRefresh)
    
    assert.True(t, needsRefresh) // 10min < 15min threshold
}
```

### 4. Concurrent Access Tests
- Multiple goroutines reading state
- Concurrent refresh triggers
- Race condition detection with -race flag

### 5. Monitor Loop Tests
- Loop starts and stops cleanly
- Refresh triggers at correct intervals
- Context cancellation stops loop
- Error recovery (single profile failure doesnt crash loop)

### 6. Profile State Transitions
```
Unknown -> Checking -> Ready
Unknown -> Checking -> Expired -> Refreshing -> Ready
Unknown -> Checking -> Expired -> Refreshing -> Error
Ready -> Expired (TTL passed)
Refreshing -> Ready (success)
Refreshing -> Error (failure)
Error -> Refreshing (retry)
```

## Logging Requirements
Each test MUST log:
- Initial state
- Action being taken
- State after action
- Time taken for operations
- Any errors with full context

## Mock Components
```go
type MockRefresher struct {
    refreshDelay time.Duration
    shouldFail   bool
    callCount    int
}

func (m *MockRefresher) Refresh(ctx context.Context, profile string) error {
    m.callCount++
    time.Sleep(m.refreshDelay)
    if m.shouldFail {
        return errors.New("mock refresh failure")
    }
    return nil
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All state transitions tested
- [ ] Concurrent access is safe (passes -race)
- [ ] Monitor loop is tested
- [ ] Mock refresher allows failure injection
- [ ] 90%+ code coverage

- internal/authpool/pool_test.go
- internal/authpool/monitor_test.go
- internal/authpool/mock_test.go
